body_txt: Cerberus (Greek Κέρβερος , "Kérberos") is the name of a three-headed canine monster from Classical Mythology belonging to the Greek god Hades . The spawn of the Monster Progenitors Typhon and Echidna, it guards the gates of The Underworld , making sure that the souls of the dead don't get out and that everyone else doesn't get in. His best known appearance is in the 12th and final labor of Heracles, better known by his Roman name of Hercules . He was instructed to drag the beast out of the Underworld, which is way easier said than done. After a fierce battle, Herc won the bout, and he was remembered as a hero of unfathomable strength note Though Cerberus was safely returned to Hades, who was, after all, Heracles' uncle . The beast would find another big break a few centuries later when Dante gave it a role in the first book of his three-part epic The Divine Comedy , where it inhabited the third circle of Hell , Gluttony. It ripped apart sinners in a giant pit of mud, though Virgil managed to tame the beast by feeding it. This particular iteration of the character placed Cerberus in a rare spot note It was commonly believed that "Kerberos" was Greek for "spotted", although this has been debunked. In Greek, the closest translation to this is "growling" or "Flesh-eating". among mythical monsters, showing up regularly in works of both Greco-Roman and Abrahamic inspiration even if Crossover Cosmology is not in play. Since then, modern fiction continues to feature Cerberus. Typically it is a three-headed Canis Major , but various other additions to his description have been made over the years. Several arts depicted it with a fourth snake head for a tail , though this has largely fallen out of favor with more recent artists. The Theogony claimed it had a whopping 50 heads, whereas Pindar stated he had twice that amount. Horace still had the last laugh though, as his Cerberus ditched the two extra dog heads in favor of a hundred snake heads , winning out by one extra head. One source even claimed that the beast had two backs ! Modern fiction has largely abandoned the idea of Cerberus having serpentine characteristics, traditionally depicting it with only the three canine heads, which are almost universally arranged horizontally. Moreover, fiction tends to be divided between depicting the heads with a Hive Mind or giving the beast a more traditional Multiple Head Case with each head having its own personality. More subversive fiction may make the creature a Big Friendly Dog underneath its fearsome exterior, rather fitting due to its relative benevolence in the original myths. Cerberi may also resemble creatures other than the traditional dog, or have a different number of heads than the traditional three, and they may fall under a higher level on the Sliding Scale of Anthropomorphism ten one may expect. Regardless of how divergent the Cerberus may be, it's still the fierce monster of legend underneath the surface, unless it isn't . Modern portrayals will also sometimes invoke Dog Stereotypes when depicting Cerberus by basing him off of a particular breed of dog. Most often, he'll resemble a stereotypically "ferocious" breed — usually a bulldog, a doberman, a rottweiler, or a pit bull — but in the more subversive portrayals, he'll be shown as something cutsier, like a chihuahua or a poodle. In some of the Denser and Wackier takes on Cerberus, his three heads will all inexplicably be composed of entirely different dog breeds. Or he may even be portrayed as a wolf-like beast . Important to note that the presence of a monstrous guardian canine is found across many mythologies; several European cultures classify their versions of the entity as the Hellhound , and as a result, Cerberus is typically identified with it. Though unlike the former, a hellhound is featured universally as an entire breed of creature. It has thus become increasingly common to depict Cerberus as not being a specific creature as it was in the original mythology, but rather as an entire species note Unlike most examples of characters being turned into species, Cerberus did have a second of its kind, that being his two-headed brother Orthrus as often as it is for works to (sometimes inaccurately ) portray these beasts as the same species across cultures. With Cerberus' attributes being a popular stylistic choice for Hellhounds that are not the monster itself, general demon hounds simply having multiple heads is not enough for this nature of depiction to qualify for this trope. The work must denote, reference, and/or acknowledge them as the Greek Cerberus. See also Classical Chimera , Harping on About Harpies , Medusa , Our Hydras Are Different , Our Manticores Are Different , and Our Sphinxes Are Different for more monsters derived from Greco-Roman legend. Sub-Trope of Mythical Motifs and Public Domain Character . Sister Trope of Hellhound . Not To Be Confused With Cerebus , the Trope Namer for the tropes Cerebus Syndrome , Knight of Cerebus , Cerebus Retcon , Cerebus Call-Back , Cerebus Rollercoaster , and Reverse Cerebus Syndrome .
